title: The Man in the Sycamore
subtitle: Zacchaeus climbs the tree that is the Cross, and the Lord looks up at him

stillness: Be still, and lay down the burden of riches.
The crowd of cares presses your soul, lest it gaze upon the light of truth.
Let the tumult of worldly things grow quiet within you.
gospel: And the Lord Jesus, entering Jericho, was passing through it,
hemmed in by no small crowd.
And behold, a man named Zacchaeus,
who was not only a tax-collector, but a chief of the tax-collectors,
And he sought to see Jesus, who he was,
and could not for the crowd, because he was small of stature.
And running ahead, he climbed up into a sycamore tree to see him,
for he was to pass that way.
And Jesus came to the place and looked up,
and said to him: Zacchaeus, make haste, come down;
for today I must abide in your house.
And he made haste and came down, and received him rejoicing.
behold: Attend to each thing as if you were present.
Behold the rich man and the tax-collector,
who, leaving behind the load of his riches and despising the gain of his frauds,
ascends the narrow gate and the strait way that leads to life.
Behold him: small in body by his stature, small in mind by his humility.
Behold the sycamore — the fig that bears no fruit, the tree the world calls foolish.
This tree is the tree of faith and of the Cross.
Its fruit is blood-red: it is Christ himself, reddened in it with his own blood.
Climb, you also, the wood where Jesus hung for you, and you shall see Jesus.
Behold the Lord stop beneath the tree, and lift up his eyes to the little man.
consider: Jericho means the world, which the Lord entered, walked through, and passed beyond in his Passion.
The world is to be passed through, for through it we must cross most swiftly,
and in no way must we halt here in disordered love, nor cling to it;
for scarcely is there anyone who is not defiled, if he is entangled in the world's affairs.
We too are small of stature, like Zacchaeus, and cannot see Jesus for the crowd.
For our own merits do not suffice, without the merit of Christ's Passion on the Cross.
This tree we must climb by faith and by meditation — otherwise we cannot see Jesus.
The blood of the mulberry, like to blood, was shown of old to sharpen the elephants for battle;
so the blood of Christ, shown to us by remembrance, sharpens us for the spiritual war.
Whoever is humbled for the sake of Jesus is made a fool before the world,
yet what is foolish to men is the wisdom of God.
Let us learn this praiseworthy folly: to leave behind the harmful wisdom.
What is more foolish in this world than to seek not what is lost,
to let go the seized to those who seize it,
to repay no injury for injuries received, and to offer the patient even more?
They who humbly choose the world's folly, these contemplate God's wisdom on high.
With the Apostle cry out: God forbid that I should glory, save in the Cross of our Lord Jesus Christ.
prayer: who looked with mercy on Zacchaeus climbing into the sycamore,
and calling him to yourself and turning aside into his house, healed him of every vice:
grant to me, little and wretched as I am,
that I may be able to climb the tree of penance,
where you may deign to look on me with the eyes of mercy,
and to call me to your grace;
granting me to prepare the house of my conscience worthily to receive you,
and through you to be wholly healed, within and without.
And you who came from heaven to seek and to save what had perished on earth,
seek me, who through my fault had perished, O good Shepherd,
and make me whole, O loving Saviour. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Ecce sycomorum; ascende et tu lignum ubi pro te pependit Jesus, et videbis Jesum.
latinJewel.english: Behold the sycamore: climb, you also, the wood where Jesus hung for you, and you shall see Jesus.
